paper.title: A runaway T-Tauri star leaving an extended trail

extraction.summary: This paper reports a runaway T-Tauri star UJT-1 (runaway T-Tauri star, RATTS), which is traveling supersonically through the interstellar medium and leaving a ~50 pc long bow shock trail. Multi-epoch astrometry yields proper motions μα cos δ = -1±1 mas/yr, μδ = -6±1 mas/yr, radial velocity -33±4 km/s, distance ~4.5 kpc (range 2-4.5 kpc). After subtracting Galactic rotation and solar motion, the peculiar velocity is 15-80 km/s at 90% confidence, with a median of ~45 km/s. The paper explicitly classifies it as a runaway star (runaway threshold ~30 km/s), but never discusses Galactic escape velocity (~500 km/s), hypervelocity stars (HVS), Galactic gravitational unbinding (Galactic-unbound), or positive total energy orbits. UJT-1's velocity is only on the order of tens of km/s, far below the Galactic escape velocity; it is merely an ordinary runaway star moving supersonically in the ISM, still gravitationally bound to the Galaxy. No HVS candidates.

candidate_groups_considered.description: UJT-1 — the only star studied in this paper. A classical TTS (Class II YSO), spectral type ~G5 III, mass ~2 M⊙, radius ~2 R⊙. Its kinematic parameters were determined from 8 epochs (1953-2021) of astrometric and optical spectroscopic observations. Peculiar velocity 15-80 km/s (90% confidence interval), median ~45 km/s; the paper classifies it as a runaway T-Tauri star (RATTS).
candidate_groups_considered.reason: UJT-1 is an ordinary runaway T-Tauri star with a peculiar velocity of only 15-80 km/s, far below the Galactic escape velocity (~500 km/s). The paper never discusses Galactic gravitational unbinding, hypervelocity stars (HVS), or positive total energy orbits. The paper merely characterizes it as a runaway star moving supersonically in the ISM (threshold ~30 km/s), which is an ordinary runaway star within the Galaxy, still gravitationally bound to the Galaxy. No HVS candidates.

source_refs.context: Section 4: Stellar run-away velocity. Peculiar velocity in the range 15 to 80 km/s at 90% confidence. ~75% of this interval is above the usual threshold (~30 km/s) for runaway stars. UJT-1 is classified as a runaway candidate, not HVS.
Section 8.1: Bona fide RATTS. UJT-1 proposed as prototype of RATTS systems. Wake extends ~50 pc, crossing lifetime ~1 Myr, consistent with typical TTS age. All discussion within Galactic context.

Introduction: explicitly frames the work as runaway T-Tauri stars (RATTS) — stars escaping their natal clouds, not the Galaxy. References other runaway star studies using Gaia data focused on early-type stars.
